title_original: ה-AI מגיע לאנטנה ומשנה את פני הרשת
description: בתערוכת MWC 2026 הנפתחת היום בברצלונה, מוצגות 33 הדגמות להטמעת בינה מלאכותית בתשתית האלחוטית של הדור השישי. "כאשר 6G יהיה עמוד השדרה של עידן ה-AI, הטלקום ישמש כמערכת העצבים של הכלכלה"
title_he: ה-AI מגיע לאנטנה ומשנה את פני הרשת
summary_he: בתערוכת MWC 2026, הוצגו 33 הדגמות של הטמעת בינה מלאכותית בתשתית האלחוטית של הדור השישי. הדבר יאפשר אוטומציה של תחזוקת הרשת ויביא להוזלת עלויות.
details_he: בתערוכת MWC 2026, הוצגו 33 הדגמות של הטמעת בינה מלאכותית בתשתית האלחוטית של הדור השישי. הדבר יאפשר אוטומציה של תחזוקת הרשת ויביא להוזלת עלויות. הארכיטקטורה החדשה, המבוססת על בינה מלאכותית, תאפשר ניהול רשת גמיש ותבצע אופטימיזציה מיידית של המשדרים והמקלטים. הדבר יאפשר גם להתמודד עם המורכבות העצומה של רשתות הדור השישי.

description: לפני קצת יותר מ-5 שנים נכנסה Tesla לשוק הישראלי. על אף שהיא הספיקה להפוך למכונית החשמלית הכי נמכרת בישראל וגם לצאת לרכבת הרים עם המחירים שלה, רק בשנת 2026 היא מתחילה פעילות טרייד-אין גם בישראל – אבל בדרך שונה. לקבל פחות על הרכב המשומש, אבל לחסוך כאב ראש עסקאות טרייד-אין בענף הרכב מאפשרות לכם לחסוך […]The postטסלה מציעה לראשונה טרייד-אין בישראלappeared first onגיקטיים.

title_original: רם מכנס מונה למנכ"ל הבא של ארבה
description: ייכנס לתפקיד ב-1 באפריל. המנכ"ל הנוכחי, קובי מרנקו, ישמש כנשיא החברה. הגדירה אסטרטגיה חדשה המרחיבה את שוקי היעד וכוללת כניסה לשוק הביטחוני. נסחרת בנסד"ק לפי שווי שוק של כ-100 מיליון דולר
title_he: רם מכנס מונה למנכ"ל הבא של ארבה

title_original: טילים פגעו בדאטה סנטר של AWS במפרץ
description: כשהדאשבורד של AWS נצבע באדום וערוצי ה-Slack בהיסטריה, הראשון שאנחנו מאשימים (לפחות בראש) זה כנראה ג'וניור תורן שדחף קוד לא בדוק לפרודקשן או איש DevOps שעשה טעות קונפיגורציה קריטית ב-DNS, אבל לפחות הפעם, זו לא אשמת הג'וניור. שירותי הענן של אמזון (AWS) קרסו במספר איזורים, לאחר שאחד מהדאטה-סנטרים של החברה באיחוד האמירויות נפגע כתוצאה מהלחימה […]The postטילים פגעו בדאטה סנטר של AWS במפרץappeared first onגיקטיים.
title_he: טילים פגעו בדאטה סנטר של AWS במפרץ

title_original: HumanMCP: A Human-Like Query Dataset for Evaluating MCP Tool Retrieval Performance
description:
arXiv:2602.23367v1 Announce Type: new 
Abstract: Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers contain a collection of thousands of open-source standardized tools, linking LLMs to external systems; however, existing datasets and benchmarks lack realistic, human-like user queries, remaining a critical gap in evaluating the tool usage and ecosystems of MCP servers. Existing datasets often do contain tool descriptions but fail to represent how different users portray their requests, leading to poor generalization and inflated reliability of certain benchmarks. This paper introduces the first large-scale MCP dataset featuring diverse, high-quality diverse user queries generated specifically to match 2800 tools across 308 MCP servers, developing on the MCP Zero dataset. Each tool is paired with multiple unique user personas that we have generated, to capture varying levels of user intent ranging from precise task requests, and ambiguous, exploratory commands, reflecting the complexity of real-world
title_he: HumanMCP: A Human-Like Query Dataset for Evaluating MCP Tool Retrieval Performance
summary_he: Researchers have created a large-scale dataset called HumanMCP, which features diverse and high-quality user queries to evaluate the performance of Model Context Protocol (MCP) tool retrieval. This dataset aims to bridge the gap in existing MCP benchmarks, which often rely on unrealistic user queries. The HumanMCP dataset includes 2800 tools across 308 MCP servers and pairs each tool with multiple unique user personas.
details_he: The HumanMCP dataset was developed to address the critical gap in evaluating MCP tool usage and ecosystems. Existing MCP datasets often contain tool descriptions but fail to represent how different users portray their requests, leading to poor generalization and inflated reliability of certain benchmarks. The researchers generated diverse user queries to match 2800 tools across 308 MCP servers, capturing varying levels of user intent ranging from precise task requests to ambiguous, exploratory commands.

title_original: The Post-Quantum Journey Begins: Enforce, See, and Evolve with Quantum-Safe SASE
description: Encrypted data has a shelf life, and for many organizations it must remain secret for years. The post-quantum risk is not a dramatic collapse of encryption, but a quieter threat: attackers harvesting encrypted traffic today so they can decrypt it in the future. That is why post-quantum readiness is increasingly a board and CEO-level responsibility,...The postThe Post-Quantum Journey Begins: Enforce, See, and Evolve with Quantum-Safe SASEappeared first onCato Networks.
title_he: The Post-Quantum Journey Begins: Enforce, See, and Evolve with Quantum-Safe SASE
summary_he: As organizations face the threat of attackers harvesting encrypted traffic to decrypt it in the future, post-quantum readiness has become a board and CEO-level responsibility, with the CISO leading execution to ensure long-term business stability, compliance expectations, and trust.
details_he: The post-quantum risk is not a dramatic collapse of encryption, but a quieter threat that requires visibility into where post-quantum cryptography protection is needed and consistent policy enforcement across users, sites, and applications. Quantum-Safe SASE provides the centralized visibility and enforcement foundation to coordinate cryptographic upgrades and apply them gradually, starting where it matters most. This is a crucial step in the rollout across a complex enterprise, which requires a practical approach to ensure the security of encrypted data that has a shelf life.

title_original: What Defenders Need to Know about Iran’s Cyber Capabilities
description: With the current Iran crisis at its peak, cyber activity is a relevant part of the threat picture alongside kinetic and political pressure. Iran’s ecosystem includes multiple clusters aligned with state entities, the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) and the Ministry of Intelligence and Security (MOIS), as well as deniable operators and “hacktivist” groups. This ecosystem supports a broad set of objectives: espionage to gain intelligence and footholds; disruption and destructive activity, including DDoS attacks, pseudo-ransomware, and data wipers to impose costs; and information operations that pair destructive activity or data leaks with coordinated online amplification. This activity is expected to intensify and broaden across the Middle East, the United States, and […]The postWhat Defenders Need to Know about Iran’s Cyber Capabilitiesappeared first onCheck Point Blog.
title_he: What Defenders Need to Know about Iran’s Cyber Capabilities
summary_he: As tensions escalate in the Iran crisis, defenders must be aware of the country's diverse cyber capabilities, which encompass espionage, disruption, and information operations. These activities are not limited to Iran's immediate region but are expected to expand globally, targeting countries perceived as adversaries. The cyber threat landscape is becoming increasingly complex, with multiple clusters and actors involved.
details_he: Iran's cyber ecosystem includes various clusters aligned with state entities, such as the IRGC and MOIS, as well as deniable operators and hacktivist groups. These clusters support a range of objectives, including espionage, disruption, and information operations. Cotton Sandstorm, an Iranian cyber actor affiliated with the IRGC, is notable for its cyber-enabled influence operations and 'fast-reaction' campaigns in response to regional events. Its tactics include website defacements, DDoS attacks, email/account hijacking, and data theft, often followed by 'hack-and-leak' style amplification using fake personas and impersonation to shape narratives.

title_original: North Korean Hackers Publish 26 npm Packages Hiding Pastebin C2 for Cross-Platform RAT
description:
Cybersecurity researchers have disclosed a new iteration of the ongoing Contagious Interview campaign, where the North Korean threat actors have published a set of 26 malicious packages to the npm registry.
The packages masquerade as developer tools, but contain functionality to extract the actual command-and-control (C2) by using seemingly harmless Pastebin content as a dead drop resolver and
title_he: North Korean Hackers Publish 26 npm Packages Hiding Pastebin C2 for Cross-Platform RAT
summary_he: North Korean hackers have been found to have published 26 malicious npm packages, which appear as developer tools but contain functionality to extract command-and-control (C2) information and drop a credential stealer and remote access trojan. The C2 infrastructure is hosted on Vercel across 31 deployments. These packages were discovered as part of the ongoing Contagious Interview campaign.
details_he: The malicious packages, tracked under the moniker StegaBin, masquerade as developer tools but contain functionality to extract C2 URLs steganographically encoded within Pastebin pastes. The loader extracts these C2 URLs by replacing characters in seemingly harmless computer science essays to spell out hidden infrastructure addresses. The packages, which include names such as argonist and bcryptance, explicitly declare legitimate packages as dependencies, likely to avoid detection. These packages also come with an install script that runs the malicious payload during installation.

title_he: ISC Stormcast For Monday, March 2nd, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9830, (Mon, Mar 2nd)
summary_he: The latest ISC Stormcast podcast for Monday, March 2nd, 2026, provides an overview of the current cybersecurity landscape. Key topics and incidents are discussed in detail, offering valuable insights for security professionals. The podcast serves as a trusted source for staying informed about emerging threats and vulnerabilities.
details_he: The ISC Stormcast podcast is a weekly series that delivers in-depth analysis and commentary on the most pressing cybersecurity issues. In this episode, experts at the SANS Internet Storm Center share their expertise on various topics, including threat intelligence, incident response, and security best practices. By tuning in to the podcast, security professionals can stay up-to-date on the latest threats and learn how to mitigate potential risks to their organizations.

title_original: ClawJacked attack let malicious websites hijack OpenClaw to steal data
description: Security researchers have disclosed a high-severity vulnerability dubbed "ClawJacked" in the popular AI agent OpenClaw that allowed a malicious website to silently bruteforce access to a locally running instance and take control over it. [...]
title_he: ClawJacked attack let malicious websites hijack OpenClaw to steal data
summary_he: Security researchers have revealed a critical vulnerability in the OpenClaw AI platform that allows malicious websites to silently gain access to a locally running instance and steal sensitive data. This issue, dubbed 'ClawJacked', was discovered by Oasis Security and has been patched in version 2026.2.26. The vulnerability takes advantage of the OpenClaw gateway's default binding to localhost and its WebSocket interface.
details_he: The vulnerability, discovered by Oasis Security, is caused by the OpenClaw gateway service binding to localhost by default and exposing a WebSocket interface. This allows a malicious website visited by an OpenClaw user to use JavaScript to silently open a connection to the local gateway and attempt authentication without triggering any warnings. The researchers found that they could brute-force the OpenClaw management password at hundreds of attempts per second without failed attempts being throttled or logged, making it possible to guess the password in a short amount of time. Once the correct password is guessed, the attacker can silently register as a trusted device, as the gateway automatically approves device pairings from localhost without requiring user confirmation.
